blocks.content: This is what the house looked like at eleven fifty-three on Saturday morning.

Four people on the beach. The sun at its highest. The ocean doing what it always does. A cooler with drinks. Cade's voice carrying across the sand with a story about something that had happened at work that was either much worse or much funnier than it sounded — everyone had stopped being sure which.
One person inside. Upstairs. In a room he would not leave again.
And one person who had just come through the back door and stood at the foot of the stairs for a moment, listening to the house.
Two hours and twenty-two minutes later, Cade knocked on Marcus's door to tell him they were thinking about driving into town for dinner.
Cade knocked again. Tried the handle. Locked.
He called through the door. Nothing.
He went downstairs and found Dean and said: hey, can you — I don't know, he won't answer and the door is locked.
Dean went up. Knocked. Called his name.

But they had all heard the argument at nine-seventeen. Hadn't they. They'd been at dinner and they'd all heard Marcus's voice through the ceiling, muffled, back and forth, heated. He was on a call, they'd thought. Work thing, probably. Or something personal.
Dean put his shoulder into the door at ten forty-one PM.
